caveats: a: BLS data cover water, sewage, and other systems rather than 221310 alone and include public employers of all sizes.
a: Advanced metering documents technical uses but not wage-weighted labor savings or the extent of existing automation.
rho: EPA's advanced-metering page establishes capabilities rather than penetration or realized labor effects.
rho: Cybersecurity evidence covers community drinking-water systems broadly and may be more stringent than some irrigation operators.
e: The Census industry definition mixes drinking-water and irrigation systems with different ownership, regulation, seasonality, and transferability.
e: The frozen firm estimate is derived from private-firm receipts but may still include mutual, cooperative, or special-purpose structures that do not fit a conventional acquisition.
s5: No exact national denominator of eligible privately controlled systems and completed control transfers was found.
s5: Strategic consolidators may make transfers more frequent in some states, while local opposition and approval processes can block or delay them.
q: Capture differs sharply among investor-owned regulated utilities, unregulated small systems, mutual water companies, and irrigation associations.
q: The estimate concerns discounted retained gross benefit and does not treat capital investment, rate base growth, or financing as labor benefit.
d5: BLS projects the broader 221300 industry, including sewage, rather than six-digit water supply and irrigation quantity.
d5: EPA's $625 billion need is a twenty-year capital requirement for public drinking-water systems, not a forecast of billed water demand or private LMM revenue.
o: This estimates persistence of an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not survival of a particular ownership structure or firm.
o: Severe drought or source loss can eliminate individual systems even while aggregate water service remains essential.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring metered, tariff, assessment, membership, capacity, or wholesale revenue for operating an essential physical water supply or irrigation system for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Government administration of irrigation districts, water and sewer construction contractors, lawn sprinkler installers, landscaping irrigation services, bottled-water retailers, captive industrial systems, and software or equipment vendors that do not operate a water system.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators of water treatment plants and water supply or irrigation systems serving external residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, or wholesale customers through pumping, treatment, storage, aqueducts, and distribution mains.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS reports 58,320 jobs in NAICS 221300, including 23.11% office and administrative support, 32.13% production, 12.88% installation and repair, 9.54% construction, 3.67% business and financial operations, and 1.65% engineering; EPA describes advanced metering uses in billing, leak detection, and resource management. Transformation: assigned high exposure to repetitive office, analytical, planning, and data-review tasks; partial exposure to operator monitoring and engineering; and low exposure to field, construction, repair, sampling, and accountable control tasks, net of existing SCADA and meter automation. Error direction: including sewage can distort the mix, and current automation may make gross task exposure overstate incremental opportunity, while small water systems may retain more manual billing and monitoring than the central case assumes.
caveats: BLS data cover water, sewage, and other systems rather than 221310 alone and include public employers of all sizes.
Advanced metering documents technical uses but not wage-weighted labor savings or the extent of existing automation.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study of private water and irrigation operators, separating already automated SCADA and metering from incremental AI and distinguishing administrative, operator, field, and maintenance tasks, would materially move the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist billing, customer service, collections, meter-data review, demand forecasting, leak and anomaly detection, maintenance planning, compliance documentation, procurement, scheduling, and engineering analysis. Licensed plant operation, sampling, field repair, construction, emergency response, safety decisions, and accountable control of treatment remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects real output for water, sewage, and other systems from 15.6 to 16.3 over ten years, a 0.5% annual rate; EPA estimates $625 billion of public drinking-water infrastructure need over twenty years, including pipe, treatment, storage, and source assets. Transformation: used roughly five years of BLS real-output growth as the central constant-quality service-demand anchor and widened it for six-digit mix, customer growth, drought, conservation, irrigation volatility, and compliance-driven treatment. Error direction: capital need can overstate delivered-water quantity and sewage inclusion can distort growth, while stricter treatment standards and new connections can raise constant-quality service demand even if gallons remain flat.
caveats: BLS projects the broader 221300 industry, including sewage, rather than six-digit water supply and irrigation quantity.
EPA's $625 billion need is a twenty-year capital requirement for public drinking-water systems, not a forecast of billed water demand or private LMM revenue.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year state or utility forecasts of constant-quality billed water volume, connections, irrigation deliveries, wholesale supply, and treatment requirements, separated by ownership and climate region, would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: Population, household formation, industrial and agricultural activity, aging-infrastructure renewal, and new treatment requirements support service demand, but conservation, leakage reduction, drought restrictions, climate variability, and declining use per customer limit delivered-water growth. Essential infrastructure needs support spending but do not directly equal water quantity.

caveats: The Census industry definition mixes drinking-water and irrigation systems with different ownership, regulation, seasonality, and transferability.
The frozen firm estimate is derived from private-firm receipts but may still include mutual, cooperative, or special-purpose structures that do not fit a conventional acquisition.
e.change_evidence: A record-level match of Census firms to state utility commissions, EPA public-water-system ownership, irrigation-district legal form, normalized EBITDA, external billing, and transferability would directly estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: Private water utilities and many irrigation-system operators deliver recurring service to external customers and can fit the lens. Eligibility is reduced by governmental or quasi-governmental entities, member or mutual systems without transferable economics, captive systems, construction-led firms, entities outside the EBITDA band, and systems whose regulated or nonprofit structure prevents a conventional control acquisition.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 221310 ($4,181,435,384) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 221310 ($15,078,667,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3492 (margins.json[22]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Utility (General) EBITDA/Sales 34.92%); revenue/employee $370,392 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 221310); class <5: 2,342 firms, avg revenue/firm $389,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $135,917 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 769 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,280,692 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $447,218 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 264 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,365,193 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,175,125 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 149 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,351,450 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,614,726 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,183,707 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,730,551 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $291,030,516 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $101,627,856 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 413

caveats: This estimates persistence of an accountable operator of the lens's kind, not survival of a particular ownership structure or firm.
Severe drought or source loss can eliminate individual systems even while aggregate water service remains essential.
o.change_evidence: Utility and customer evidence quantifying municipal takeovers, well or recycled-water self-supply, decentralized treatment, consolidation, service abandonment, and fully remote operating models would materially move the surviving operator-required share.
o.rationale: Customers still require an accountable operator to source, treat, test, pump, store, distribute, bill, maintain, secure, and respond to failures in a physical water system. AI, automation, conservation, and self-service billing change workflows but do not eliminate regulated operational responsibility; displacement is limited to municipalization, customer self-supply, consolidation, or loss of source water.

caveats: Capture differs sharply among investor-owned regulated utilities, unregulated small systems, mutual water companies, and irrigation associations.
The estimate concerns discounted retained gross benefit and does not treat capital investment, rate base growth, or financing as labor benefit.
q.change_evidence: State rate-case and contract evidence on operating-expense trackers, sharing mechanisms, allowed returns, rate lags, fixed versus volumetric charges, and post-efficiency revenue requirements would materially refine retention.
q.rationale: Natural-monopoly territories, essential service, tariff lags, fixed charges, and customer switching difficulty can preserve near-term implemented benefits. Over five years, regulated rate cases, public or member governance, affordability pressure, cost-of-service reviews, conservation adjustments, and wholesale or irrigation negotiations can return part of productivity gains to customers.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: EPA reports that advanced metering provides frequent accurate usage data for billing, leak detection, and resource management, while its water-sector cybersecurity guidance documents reliance on IT and operational technology and risks to treatment, distribution, pumps, valves, and chemical control. Transformation: translated available digital use cases into the share of exposed tasks implemented over five years and discounted for legacy integration, capital, skills, safety validation, cybersecurity, and public-health responsibility. Error direction: capability evidence can overstate LMM deployment and labor realization, while infrastructure funding and packaged utility software could accelerate adoption beyond the central case.
caveats: EPA's advanced-metering page establishes capabilities rather than penetration or realized labor effects.
Cybersecurity evidence covers community drinking-water systems broadly and may be more stringent than some irrigation operators.
rho.change_evidence: Water-utility deployment studies reporting baseline staffing, implementation cost, validated accuracy, cyber controls, hours avoided, false alarms, leakage reductions, and adoption by system size would materially refine implementation.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should advance in office workflows, meter analytics, leak detection, maintenance planning, compliance support, and forecasting. Realization is constrained by fragmented small-system data, old infrastructure and control systems, limited capital and technical staff, procurement cycles, validation, public-health accountability, cybersecurity, and the need for human override and field response.

caveats: No exact national denominator of eligible privately controlled systems and completed control transfers was found.
Strategic consolidators may make transfers more frequent in some states, while local opposition and approval processes can block or delay them.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year state public-utility-commission dockets and transaction databases matched to system ownership, control transfer, EBITDA, water rights, and post-close operating continuity would materially move the probability.
s5.rationale: Investor-owned systems can transfer through strategic consolidation or owner succession, but utility sales often require regulatory, municipal, customer, lender, or water-right approvals and many mutual or district structures are not conventionally saleable. Completed qualifying transfers therefore plausibly trail general small-business intentions.

principal_driver: Essential recurring water service and aging distribution and treatment infrastructure sustain demand and operational need.
principal_weakness: Regulation, public or mutual ownership, capital intensity, and transaction approvals constrain both benefit retention and acquisition availability.

sources.supports: Exact industry scope, system components, uses, exclusions, and employer-establishment context.

sources.supports: Operational-technology dependence, public-health consequences, cyber risk, and required human risk and response controls.

title: Water Supply and Irrigation Systems
